Theorem 4.3.12.

Suppose that \(\mathbf f:D\subset \bbR^n\mapsto E\subset \bbR^m\) is differentiable at \(\bx_0\text{,}\) with \(\by_0=\mathbf f(\bx_0) \in E\text{,}\) and \(\mathbf g:E \mapsto \bbR^l\) is differentiable at \(\by_0\text{,}\) then \(\mathbf g\circ \mathbf f\) is differentiable at \(\bx_0\text{,}\) with its Jacobian derivative \([D(\mathbf g\circ \mathbf f) (\bx_0)]\) given by the matrix product \([D\mathbf g (\by_0)][D\mathbf f(\bx_0)]\text{.}\)
In component notation, this is
\begin{equation*} D_{i}(g_{k}\circ f)(\bx_{0})=\sum_{j=1}^{m}D_{j}g_{k} \circ f (\bx_{0}) D_{i}f_{j}(\bx_{0})= \sum_{j=1}^{m} D_{j}g_{k} ( f (\bx_{0})) D_{i}f_{j}(\bx_{0}), \end{equation*}
or
\begin{equation*} \frac{\partial (g_{k}\circ f)}{\partial x_{i}}(\bx_{0})=\sum_{j=1}^{m} \frac{\partial g_{k}}{\partial y_{j}}( f (\bx_{0})) \frac{\partial f_{j}}{\partial x_{i}}(\bx_{0}). \end{equation*}
Note that we use a parenthesis as a delimiter to delineate the order of operations between composition and differentiation. Similar usage appears often such as in
\begin{equation*} \frac{\partial \left[ f(x^{2}, y)\right]}{\partial x}= 2x \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}(x^{2}, y). \end{equation*}

Proof.

in-context
